I Feel Like We Need To Be Less Precious About Adaptations. Yes sticking to the source material is important but books, comics etc are a different medium from TV and some stuff doesn't translate. One recent example that springs to mind is seeing people annoyed that the three Dragonseeds didn't fight at the Gullet in the latest episode of House Of The Dragon as in the book. But the sheer cost and logistical challenge of doing that sequence with six dragons would be crazy. Plus it would completely remove any tension since Seasmoke, Vermithor, and Silverwing are fully grown monsters and practically untouchable while Vermax and Moondancer are still young and vulnerable. Not to mention it would make the scene too crowded and pull focus from the POV characters. It's easy to write a few lines in a book saying this person was here when this thing happened, but actually visualising it and bringing it to life onscreen is entirely different. And to be honest, I think if you don't read the books first, you're better able to enjoy the adaptation for what it is. If an adaptation is genuinely poor in its execution, then criticising it for not following the source material makes sense. But going in expecting a page-for-page copy is just unrealistic.

Craig Ferguson was a genius that deserves more acclaim. Host of the Late Late show from 2005 to end of 2014, Craig at the time had a very niche fanbase. His humor was very unique. When he went off the air his dedicated fans quietly said goodbye. Fast forward a decade later from his departure, rewatching old episodes his humor has not aged and holds up to this day. Change my mind. Craig was an absolute genius and no one has ever been able to replicate his humor or touch what he created. It was fun while it lasted. Still miss him to this day and his awkward improv and dark sense of humor and think he should be remembered as one of the greats of late night along with Letterman and Carson.

TV spinoffs as sequel series. After TV spinoffs that take place in parallel spacetime as their mother series we're now focusing on the ones who are sequel series. They can start where the original series left off like Joey with Friends, Frasier with Cheers, or so many Disney Channel/XD and Nickelodeon series such as The Suite Life on Deck with The Suite Life of Zack and Cody, Cory in the House with That's So Raven. They can also be set years after the original show ended such as Fuller House (2015-20) also set 20 years after the end of Full House (1987-95), Girl Meets World (2014-17) which began 14 years after the end of Boy Meets World (1993-2000). Can you give some other examples of sequel spinoff series?

I love Daisy Jones and The Six. Am I the only one who binge-watched Daisy Jones & The Six because I absolutely loved it? I didn't love the cheating storyline, it was uncomfortable and completely messed up, but honestly isn't that exactly what we the audience are supposed to feel? It puts us on an emotional rollercoaster forcing us to view the chaos from a third-person perspective then shifting to Daisy's point of view, Camila's, Billy's, and everyone else's. It was messy, toxic, and insane, but it did exactly what it needed to do. It exposed the raw behind-the-scenes reality and made the audience feel those same erratic emotions. And the entire cast is absolutely perfect. Camila especially is mesmerizing. The soundtrack is spectacular, I still have the songs on repeat.

I struggle to finish TV series because ending them feels like losing them. I've noticed I have this weird problem with TV shows that I really love. When I get really attached to a show and its characters, I sometimes don't want to finish it. It feels like once I watch the last episode that's it. The story is over and the characters are just gone and eventually it'll just become another show I watched. For some reason if I leave the last episode unwatched the show stays in my mind for much longer. I keep thinking about the characters and what might happen next. It feels like it's still there. The problem is that sometimes I leave it for too long. There was a show I absolutely loved back in 2016 and I still haven't watched the last episode. Back then I was really emotionally invested in it but now almost 10 years later I'm not nearly as interested. If I had watched that last episode back then it probably would've meant a lot to me. Now it probably wouldn't hit the same.
The solution is to watch shows you don't like so you can get it dead and buried by watching the finale.
Not finishing is also ending them though

There is a phrase which comes from Star Trek TNG - the show grew a beard. It refers to Riker growing a beard for season 3 but is used as a metaphor for when the show grew into itself - it took a while to work out what it could be before it could become. Many shows which are now massive hits would not have got through this stage if Netflix was in charge. In the UK shows like Top Gear, Blackadder, Red Dwarf and others would not have carried on past the first season or two unless someone who saw potential gave them a chance to grow. The metrics are unforgiving and have no judgement. Netflix needs to put back in an experienced human into the cycle to let shows with potential carry on even if season 1 is only so-so.
column3: strong analysis — uses Star Trek TNG metaphor and cites specific UK shows as evidence for argument about Netflix metrics

I would agree with you on Maya Rudolph on The Good Place. My acclaimed performance I hate pick would be Hugh Jackman in Les Miserables. Everybody always knocks poor Russell Crowe for a weak singing voice, but for me Hugh Jackman is so much worse -- he overacts, he oversings, he's vocally utterly miscast (he's a baritone straining for a lyric tenor range), and the job he does belting Bring Him Home is a crime against musical theatre. By comparison, Russell Crowe as Javert is beautifully cast in terms of acting and looking the character -- he's subtle and restrained and true to the character, and his singing voice, while not fabulous, is serviceable, especially considering he was forced to film live. And while we're on the subject, director Tom Hooper's decision to have the cast record the music live was so unfair to the performers -- almost everyone on the film shredded their vocal cords. The people who suffer most from this are Jackman, Crowe, Seyfried, and Bonham Carter.
column3: detailed comparative argument with vocal range analysis and specific director decision cited

Better Call Saul was such a rewarding experience, in that it forces you to put the pieces together instead of relying on flashbacks; a character will be contemplating their next move, then their whole demeanor will change after seeing a cockroach scuttling across a nearby pipe. If you've paid attention, you'll realize what connection the character's just made, and infer where they're likely heading next. All of this is conveyed without clumsy flashbacks or Netflix-style second screen dialogue, told purely through facial expressions, body language, and cinematography.
column3: cites specific scene (cockroach) to argue BCS communicates through visual storytelling rather than exposition

column1: The wire. When the main character is the city and then you show how every facet of life interacts with the city. It changes how you see everything else in every other show. I can't watch cop shows anymore, I can't watch shows about regular people because I question everything. What's the school system like? What's the political landscape? How do those affect how the police do their jobs? How do the criminals react to the police doing their job? No other show has ever shown all the pieces and been able to stay true and gritty
column3: argues The Wire's structural innovation (city as protagonist) changes how you evaluate all other TV

column1: I firmly believe that S1 got Matthew McConaughey the Oscar the next year. Of course it wasn't eligible, but it showed everyone that he had insane chops, so they knew a good film performance wasn't a fluke.
column3: makes specific causal argument about TD S1's effect on McConaughey's career trajectory

column1: The attention to details and the respect for real physics really highlights how many sci fi shows could put in some more effort. Plus it's just a solid story with some great characters
column3: argues The Expanse's physics realism exposes a craft gap in other sci-fi shows

The Sarah Jane Adventures and Torchwood crossing over via Doctor Who. I know it's three shows technically but I always love that the Doctor Who universe enabled a show made for kids to exist in the same space as a show where the second episode was a alien gas that possessed people and consumed others by making them orgasm.
column3: notes tonal contrast between child-oriented Sarah Jane and adult Torchwood enabled by shared Doctor Who universe

column1: The one that never happened. Picket Fences and The X-Files. They ran back to back back in the day but on opposite Networks. They wrote a two-part episode that started in one and ended in the other. Both networks had a fit and the crossover was canceled. Both episodes were changed to be self-contained on their respective shows. If you watch the episodes together you can see where they would have crossed over.
column3: documents a specific failed crossover with verifiable historical details about network conflict

column1: Should streamers do more to promote smaller shows instead of relying on fans? Fans can definitely help... But the survival of a show should not depend on fans constantly posting, streaming, tagging, and trying to convince people to give it a chance. At a certain point, that should be the job of the platform or studio. What do you think?
column3: OP discussion post asking community opinion on streamer promotion responsibility

column1: If a show has an audience of 12,000 people naturally, compared to a show with a natural audience of 36,000 people, it might make more sense for the company to build the audience of the bigger show... Streamers don't typically care if they have to cancel a show, they know that not every single show can be a major audience hit. The reality is that added additional advertising can cost them more money may make those smaller shows even less attractive because they require more resources to support.
column3: structured business logic argument about platform marketing ROI and audience-size tradeoffs; numbers explicitly hypothetical but reasoning is structured

column1: Dan Povenmire did that with Phineas and Ferb: Candace against the universe and with the revival. Disney barely did any marketing. It helped a lot. His daughters got him into tik tok and he still sometimes promotes his shows.
column3: specific verifiable example: Dan Povenmire self-promoting Phineas and Ferb Candace Against the Universe on TikTok due to Disney's minimal marketing

column1: Leaving the fate of non-IP shows up to the algorithm, fan word of mouth, and God is a recipe for failure nine times out of ten... shows become more expensive when they hit a third season. From the outside, it comes off as streamers willing to sandbag anything that's not going to reach 11 bajillion minutes in its first weekend; they save the marketing money today and don't have to renegotiate cast contracts down the road.
column3: structured argument citing known industry practice: S3 costs rise due to cast contract renegotiations as motive for undermarketing smaller shows

column1: No. Traditional marketing is a double edged sword it's expensive so you could be spending lots of money to grow a show while also raising the bar to what makes it a success... Small shows get their chance on release to catch people's eye and that's historically been enough.
column3: argues against additional promotion with reasoning but no specific show data; asserts historical norm without evidence

column1: platforms definitely need to step up more, it's wild how they'll spend millions on a show then barely market it while expecting fans to do all the heavy lifting... fans shouldn't have to become unpaid marketing teams just to keep something alive
column3: emotional agreement with OP; frustration-driven with no evidence or analysis

column1: The lady who voices Marge Simpson is 75. Kenny is 63, so potentially he can pump another decade out if he remains healthy.

column1: I remember Nickelodeon hyping up the primetime premiere of Spongebob's House Party during the Summer of 2002. It was part of Nicktoons Summer Beach House (2002-2003) where they created special segments of various Nickelodeon characters interacting with each other. They did something similar with Nickmas which had NickToons Characters parodying classic Christmas Specials. I miss when Nickelodeon used to treat their animated characters like royalty before the Snyder/Kidcom Era.
column3: structured nostalgic argument citing specific verifiable programming blocks: Spongebob's House Party (2002) Nicktoons Summer Beach House Nickmas and the Snyder/Kidcom Era

column1: Its less that Nick has fallen from grace and more that cable tv is now a feeder for streaming. Nicks gonna make spongebob because spongebob sells ads and sells paramount plus. Same with Cartoon Network and Teen Titans Go or Adult Swim and Rick and Morty. Toonami might be the only exception. Just look at how rerun heavy the schedule of cable channels now are.
column3: structured cable ecosystem argument with specific named examples: Paramount+ Cartoon Network Teen Titans Go Adult Swim Rick and Morty Toonami

column1: The Patrick Star Show is actually very creative. The second season is much better bc they made it storyboard driven instead of writing scripts. Its also one of the few cartoons that lets animators make a cartoon. Most other cartoons are trying to be live action with less cartoony stuff.
column3: specific production claim: S2 shift from script-driven to storyboard-driven production; contrasts with broader industry trend toward live-action-adjacent style

column1: Its honestly depressing that 15 years later the CN renaissance trio of Regular Show Gumball and Adventure Time all have their own reboots coming out within the same year. This generation is getting the past's reheated lunches because executives have become so risk averse.
column3: names three specific CN renaissance shows (Regular Show Gumball Adventure Time) all simultaneously getting reboots as evidence for executive risk-aversion thesis

column1: When Season 10 introduced the wacky faces and completely deviated from the original style of humor which even the Paul Tibbitt era retained I gave up on it completely. I still think there was good in Seasons 4-9 despite being a downgrade from 1-3. If you cherry pick certain episodes from those seasons you won't go wrong.
column3: detailed quality analysis with specific season breakdowns (1-3 vs 4-9 vs 10+) and named stylistic change at S10; also references Paul Tibbitt era

column1: Man almost 30 years and Nick has fallen so far from grace.
column3: bold dismissive claim about Nickelodeon's decline with no specific evidence

column1: Spongebob hasn't been good since season 5 before the original movie came out. Sigh.
column3: asserts quality cutoff at season 5 without analysis; "Sigh" signals emotional rather than argued position

column1: I would be less surprised if Disney cancels The Simpsons than if Nickelodeon cancels SpongeBob.

column1: Tom Kenny's voice has already been showing its age for the last couple of years. I'm surprised he still has the stamina for spongebob honestly.

column1: I love a lot of the modern episodes of SpongeBob. My favorite seasons are 3 4 5 11 and 15. I think the Patrick Show is alright. It took awhile to grow on me and it got better as it went. Super Stars won me over. That episode is freaking hilarious.

column1: I don't care if these shows keep getting shoveled out but I just wish Avatar was given half the amount of love SpongeBob gets from these executives.

column1: Is there a show that limped to the finish line you haven't seen discussed much? American Housewife (2016) was a refreshing sitcom when it first started airing... Julia Butters left after breaking out with Once Upon a Time in Hollywood. Carly Hughes walked away citing harassment and a toxic working environment. Ali Wong only appeared in a brief scene per episode via FaceTime. Franklin began to take over the series heading towards the end in a way that almost echoed Urkel.

column1: The Goldbergs was really interesting until they ran out of IRL Adam's home movies to base episodes off. I punched out on the last season or two.

column1: I agree about the later seasons. The first few seasons were so good too. Reminded me an 80s version of The Wonder Years with its narration.

column1: I lost count of how many times Pope did something that 100% should have gotten him executed by the other main characters only for him to be forgiven and kept around so he could inevitably do it all again 2-3 episodes later.

column1: The Drew Carey Show. It went from a perfectly average sitcom to something offbeat and surreal then just seemed to be waiting for the hourglass to run out by the last few seasons. It didn't even have a clever opening sequence by the end and that was such a huge part of the show.
column3: traces three-stage quality arc of The Drew Carey Show; cites loss of opening sequence as specific measurable indicator of declining production investment

column1: Between The Lions was a huge show that lost its funding. Mississippi Public Broadcast gave it enough funding for 6 more seasons but several things had to be cut. This resulted in several years of a lesser version airing.
column3: specific verifiable production history: Mississippi Public Broadcast as funder enabling 6-season extension with budget constraints

column1: Castle. The show started to limp once the will-they-won't-they tension got resolved... the two lead actors apparently near the end hated each other and a lot of scenes had to be written around that so they weren't in the same physical space together much.
column3: identifies two verifiable causes of Castle's decline: narrative (tension resolution) and production (Stana Katic/Nathan Fillion reported feud requiring rewritten scenes)

column1: I agree the last 2 seasons were a step downwards but idk about limping to the finish line. Its not like say Westworld where everyone involved seemed to be checking out by the final season.
column3: distinguishes degree of decline between The Boys and Westworld as named comparison; argues Westworld is a more extreme example of the phenomenon

column1: Definitely community in every sense except the writing. Lost Chevy Chase because he's an asshole Donald Glover left for his career Yvette Nicole-Brown left I think to take care of her dad all while the show's episode order and budget was cut down. The show would have been canceled if not for Yahoo TV which eked out I think two seasons.
column3: detailed Community behind-the-scenes account with named verifiable cast departures (Chevy Chase Donald Glover Yvette Nicole Brown) and Yahoo TV rescue

column1: I have a memory of the Adam actor taking himself a little too seriously in the later seasons. I think he was a little jelly of all the attention Tony Gentile was getting but even towards the end Barry was a parody of himself. Adam F. was/is a solid television producer. looks like he's got a doc on the Police Academy movies coming out soon.
column3: references Adam F. Goldberg (verifiable producer) Tony Gentile (verifiable actor) and Police Academy documentary; argues cast jealousy dynamics contributed to Barry's flanderization

column1: The 100. That final season wasn't just bad it seemed to be deliberately written as a fuck you to its most loyal viewers.
